body:
As you know, we're evaluating whether Storage and Pricing should consolidate under a single management lead or remain separate. The reporting structure argument for merging is straightforward — cleaner line into the commercial book. The counter is the regulatory complexity around Storage, and whether combining the groups changes our compliance footprint in ways we haven't fully mapped.

I need input from each of you on the operational implications, but specifically, Drew, your view on the regulatory side is critical here. Before we move forward with any recommendation, I need to understand whether a merger introduces new compliance considerations or changes our existing risk profile around Storage.

Give me your read by end of week. I want to get a recommendation to the executive team before we get too deep into Q4 planning.

Dan

Danny -

Short answer: a merger of the Storage and Pricing groups under a single management lead does not, in my view, materially change our regulatory compliance footprint. The storage assets and their attendant FERC obligations exist independent of org structure. The tariff compliance, rate design, and contract administration requirements remain the same whether one VP oversees both or two do.

That said, two considerations worth flagging before you take a recommendation up:

1. The regulatory complexity around Storage is less about org structure and more about the ongoing tariff work and any pending or anticipated FERC proceedings. If Storage's regulatory load is particularly active right now — pending rate cases, tariff amendments, compliance filings — there's an argument for keeping dedicated focus rather than folding it into a broader commercial portfolio. But that's a workload management issue, not a compliance risk issue.

2. If the consolidated lead doesn't have a strong working knowledge of FERC storage regulations, you'd want to ensure the compliance function itself remains appropriately resourced and that the regulatory expertise is retained somewhere in the org chart. The risk isn't the merger per se — it's whether the consolidated structure inadvertently disperses that expertise.

My recommendation: the merger is fine from a regulatory standpoint, provided you address the expertise continuity point above. Happy to discuss further if you want to dig into specifics.

df

Got it. That's the clarity I needed.

The compliance footprint question is answered. The expertise continuity point is valid and will be baked into whatever structure we recommend. I'll take this forward with the caveat that whoever lands the consolidated lead needs to have or have access to solid FERC storage knowledge — not just commercial instincts.

I'll have a recommendation to the exec team by end of next week. If anything changes on the regulatory side — new filings, active proceedings — flag it sooner.

Dan

Understood. I'll flag anything that comes up on the regulatory side. Good luck with the exec recommendation.

df
